data.title: The Real Crypto World Through the Eyes of an Influential Person After Reviewing 2000 Projects
data.description:
Guest: Chase, former Binance listing manager; Podcast source: Class Representative at Attention; Compiled by: Deep Tide

Broadcast Date: February 10, 2026

Guest: Chase, former Listing Manager at Binance. During his tenure, he handled approximately 1000-2000 projects and personally oversaw the token launches of about 100 projects. The data he handled was of extremely high quality, representing a top-level perspective in the industry.

Core Insight: He believes the cryptocurrency world (especially the exchange environment) is a "non-dramatic" financial battlefield. Unlike the stock market, which is still cloaked in financial reports, it is rife with liquidity games and market maker manipulation. He observed that Bitcoin's candlestick charts can be manipulated by market makers to create perfect "artificial patterns" to declare their dominance.

Billion Dollar Chart (CFD) patterns can be drawn by market makers to claim perfect "artificial patterns."

Billion Dollar Chart (CFD) patterns can be manipulated to claim perfect dominance.

... In this market, information, traffic, attention, and even insider information are assets in themselves; as long as they have liquidity, they can be priced. * **Key Insights:** Macroeconomics and Crypto Valuation Logic: The Truth Behind the US Stock Market Bull Run: From the dot-com bubble to the present, the S&P 500 has been in a long-term bull market, but this may not be the case when compared to gold. The core reason for this phenomenon is the continuous expansion of the money supply. * **Asset Price Support:** Newly added money must find asset carriers. The growth of goods and services (GDP) cannot keep up with the rate of money issuance and credit growth. Excess money, besides forming bubbles, must flow into new asset classes. Cryptocurrencies, due to their low-cost issuance and lack of centralized verification, have become an excellent container for global overflow liquidity. • Cryptocurrency Valuation System: Unlike traditional finance which focuses on cash flow, the short-to-medium-term performance of cryptocurrency assets is determined by three factors: 1. Liquidity; 2. Attention/Traffic; 3. Tokenomics. Market Manipulation and "Market Maker" Mentality. • Can Bitcoin be manipulated? Yes. Chase observed Bitcoin rising for seven consecutive days with almost identical magnitude and trading volume in a certain week, followed by a sharp drop. This wasn't natural volatility, but rather a signal from large financial groups (market makers/market manipulators) to the market – "I control the rhythm; those who understand follow, those who don't get eaten up." • Everything can be priced: In the crypto world, information, traffic, and even insider information (such as Polymarket prediction markets) can be assetized. When most people reach a "consensus," it's often the moment for a reverse harvest. • A market without pretense: Exchanges like Binance vividly demonstrate what naked trading is. There are value investors here, but more often, there are market makers who see through liquidity and short-term profits; this is a financial battlefield where "the act is over."

  1. Frontier Trends: The Combination of AI Agents and Crypto

• The Measurement Challenge of AI: In the future, the economic scale generated by AI agents will exceed that of humans, but the existing GDP system cannot measure the micro-transactions of AI (API calls, data exchanges).

• Crypto as Infrastructure: Crypto is a financial infrastructure naturally suited for AI. It can provide measurement and settlement accurate to every tiny transaction, without the cumbersome trust processes of the banking system. Crypto may be for AI, not for humans.

  1. Industry Insights and Wealth Opportunities

• The Huge Profits of Stablecoins: Tron carries a large amount of USDT. Tether (the issuer of USDT) earns more in a year (approximately $15 billion) than the combined losses of OpenAI and Anthropic. This is a risk-free arbitrage model relying on the interest rate differential between US Treasury bonds, making it the industry's true "cash cow."

• Capital Equality: Crypto's biggest change in production relations lies in "capital equality." Regardless of who you are, as long as you meet the criteria, your funds can obtain equal returns (such as DeFi yields), breaking the traditional banks' discriminatory treatment of retail investors and high-net-worth clients.

• Capital Equality: Crypto's biggest change in production relations lies in "capital equality." Regardless of who you are, as long as you meet the criteria, your funds can obtain equal returns (such as DeFi yields), breaking the traditional banks' differential treatment of retail investors and high-net-worth clients.

• Career Advice:

  ◦ Regarding getting rich quick: This industry does indeed have a "spiritually rich" bonus; ordinary people who work hard (like taking advantage of airdrops) may earn more than in traditional manufacturing, but ultimately the big money flows to "Nascent Soul cultivators" (top players) or is redistributed.

  ◦ Advice for newcomers: First, understand two things—Bitcoin (as an asset allocation) and stablecoins (as a payment revolution). Don't just focus on speculation; see the opportunities brought about by the transformation of the payment system.

  ◦ 2026 Prediction: Bitcoin will rise, but possibly in ways you wouldn't expect (through pricing all information and liquidity games).

Opening and Guest Introduction: The Power of the Listing Manager at Binance

Class Representative: Hello everyone, welcome Chase, a very mysterious and powerful figure in the crypto world. Chase, please introduce yourself.

Chase: Hello everyone, I used to be a powerful figure, but now I'm down on my luck, haha. I'm leaving to pursue my dreams, giving up enormous power. Hello everyone, I'm Chase. As the class representative mentioned, my most recent job was as a Listing Manager at Binance, the largest cryptocurrency exchange in the crypto world. This job mainly helps the founders mint their own tokens. Between the time I joined and the time I left Binance, its annual trading volume had reached the level of Nasdaq, so it has a significant scale.

Class Representative: Let me explain your job for everyone.

These people issue a coin, but if they want to trade it on Binance, which has the trading volume of Nasdaq, it has to go through your team. My understanding is, you might not be able to say it like that, but it's roughly similar to the position of the Securities and Exchange Commission in deciding whether a company can go public. So all the founders are very... hh

Chase: There are similarities in function, but legally you can't define it like that.

Class Representative: Okay, please continue. How big is your team? And what is your role in it?

Chase: Our team has about a dozen people. My official title here is Listing Building Manager. We used to have 4 people, now there are fewer. There are 4 people who can really approach and talk to everyone externally, interpret everyone's doubts, guide everyone, and tell everyone how to do things A, B, C, and D. ...>

That's roughly it.

Class Representative: Wow, I think your background contains several very important pieces of information. First, you definitely look at a lot of projects; you're probably one of the people in the world who looks at the most crypto projects, because it's your job. And because you do have real power—we won't shy away from this—especially since you're leaving soon, there's no conflict of interest anymore. So the founders value you highly; the core resources and information you get are very firsthand and very authentic. This includes your ability to form judgments. Third, one of your jobs involves communicating with the industry, so you've definitely formed your own opinions and insights. I think these aspects give you a unique position. So, what do you think you can talk about today?

Chase: I think the most I can share is what the model looks like after I input so much data. And I think my data is relatively high-quality data, so it shouldn't be garbage.

I can now share the highest quality data with you all very honestly. From the time I joined the company until last year, a total of about two and a half years, I reviewed approximately 1,000 to 2,000 projects. Of the projects I supported last year, about 100 actually launched their tokens successfully. [Class Representative: Tell us what this number means, both in terms of project review and project launches.] Chase: You can understand project review as similar to how VCs review projects. You need to look at the deck, you need to look at the team, you need to talk to them, call them, and then make your own judgments—that's the basics. I think a VC reviewing 200 projects a year is pretty good, that's quite impressive. You also need to constantly communicate with the founders. But in just over two years, I reviewed more than 1,000, with 100 launching last year alone. What does 100 mean? When I first joined Binance, they only had about twenty new listings a year, the whole company only had about twenty. Then the second year they had fifty or sixty, and by the third year it was 25. Last year, I alone had 100. Then the bull market came. How many did you have in total? Around 250 or 300, I guess.

Class Representative: Anyway, you alone accounted for almost half.

Chase: 1/3. No, no, about 1/3. Because there are only four of us in total. The success rate of this selection is about 5% to 10%.

Crypto's Worldview: Tertiary Industry and AI Infrastructure

Chase: I'd like you to help everyone understand this world. How do many people who may not understand Crypto understand this world?

Chase: I'd like you to help everyone understand this world first. First, you can think of it as a virtual world. This world has its own economic cycle mechanism. If you think of it as a country, then it needs the US dollar as liquidity support. But its output, its export, is its service. It only has services, only the tertiary sector. What does its primary sector, agriculture, need? It needs computing power and electricity.

Class Representative: How do you understand this sentence? What does it mean that the output is the tertiary sector? Because we often say things like, can we use Bitcoin to buy pizza? That's not it.

Chase: Why do I say it's the tertiary sector? Because although Bitcoin has a large market share, 50% or 60%, it's not the core of this industry. We're talking about places where bubbles can be blown up; it can serve as a store of value, but it can't provide many services. A store of value is actually what we call liquidity support. Why do I call it the tertiary sector?

This industry, for example, since Ethereum emerged, many smart contracts can enable certain functions, such as DeFi, which can have a significant impact on the traditional financial industry. And I remember someone saying a few weeks ago that within the next two years, around 2026 and 2027, almost all the mainstream Wall Street institutions will be on-chain. Class Representative: Regarding DeFi, we previously invited Richard Youzhong (founder of Huma Finance) to our channel to talk about his projects. Basically, it helps companies already engaged in trade to reduce financial friction, whether it's currency exchange or lending, helping them reduce friction and improve efficiency. It's a very obvious point where a service brings value to traditional or non-crypto sectors. Chase: Yes. Class Representative: Could you list some of the main points you see? Chase: You just mentioned where this industry provides services and value, which is correct. But besides that, this industry has something very special about it. For example, flash loans. Most people probably haven't heard of them. What does it mean? For instance, if you go to someone or a bank to borrow money, you either need collateral, right? Or you need a very high credit score or something like that. You need something for the bank to lend to you. The ability of flash loans is that you can complete the lending and collection without any collateral, and without the bank taking any risk. Almost all cryptocurrencies related to payments can enable this. Chase: The most important thing, and what I might write about soon, is a paper related to AI. Let's say, five years from now, if AI continues to develop like we have now with scaling laws, the number of AI agents will definitely exceed the population, right? Based on this assumption, how do you measure the economic behavior generated between AI agents? You call its API, FINE, and you're currently using a subscription model. But often, I might only need to access a lot of long-tail data, for example, data that only you have, or data that only I have. How do you settle a payment with just one call? Right? The amount of data obtained is extremely small, and the existing banking system can't handle the settlement. All these economic activities need to be measured first. Secondly, everyone needs to trust the transaction for it to succeed. This is where Crypto can enable or collaborate with AI. This is also why many people in the industry think that Crypto itself might not be for human use; we designed it this way so that it might be for AI in the future.

Class Representative: When I interviewed Youzhong, he also mentioned something I still remember: he believes AI is a productive force, and Crypto is a production relation.

Chase: Actually, Crypto also has both productive forces and production relations.

But crypto can truly change production relations because, broadly speaking, on a theoretical level, it involves the concept of capital equality. Imagine you live in mainland China; you're used to Alipay and Yu'ebao, right? It's natural for your money to automatically accrue interest there. But in North America or Europe, you might have to go to a bank, where there are eligibility requirements, and the interest rates are very low. Crypto completely equalizes the world. Money is money; I don't care whose money it is. As long as you deposit it and meet my standards, you can get the same returns. Macro Perspective: US Stocks, Gold, and the Illusion of Currency Class Representative: As mentioned earlier, payment itself is a large system, and crypto can provide a lot of value within it. Changing production relations is another value. Are there any other points where, in your opinion, crypto offers significant value? Chase: To put it more realistically, it can help countries with excessive liquidity absorb more liquidity without affecting inflation. Class Representative: I understand, it's a store of value. Do you think there's a significant difference between this and gold? Chase: That's an excellent question. Look at the link I shared with you; there's a screenshot there. What is this screenshot? First, let me ask you a question: do you think the US stock market, from the Dot-com Bubble to now, is in a bear market or a bull market? Class Representative: It's a bull market. Chase: Yes, if you take the S&P 500 for example, it's a bull market. But for gold, it's a bear market. The chart I showed you is illustrating this point. Class Representative: Right. Interesting. It's saying that the money you printed drove the growth of the S&P 500, but it still didn't surpass the growth rate of gold, which is an inflation hedge. Wow. So the S&P is even more... Chase: This is research by Lyn Alden, a very famous author. I can give you the link later. Class Representative: Interesting, interesting, interesting. This is a huge change in my understanding of investing. Before, my flawed formal investment theory, which was hindered by economics, made me think that stock prices were the discounted value of future income. That makes sense, but I later realized why everyone said the bubble in the US stock market was about to burst, and that it would crash every 10 years, but it didn't happen. Why? I later realized it's because when too much money is printed, there's nowhere to go. Before, people couldn't create so much money, but now that so much money has been created, it has to go somewhere. Chase: Yes, that's right. You want our goods and services to grow, say, by 5% or a few percent annually, right? Your money supply, including your credit growth, completely exceeds that rate. So, besides becoming a bubble, your excess money needs to find new services or assets to price. Why new assets to price? Because in the crypto world, you can continuously issue assets at very low cost. And with these warrants, you don't need to go to a central institution to verify them. So, as long as people believe your asset has value, new liquidity will come in and absorb that liquidity. Class Representative: You just need to verify it beforehand. Chase: Yes, you just need to judge it yourself. Class Representative: No, not just you! You! Chase: Oh~ me personally, right? You! No, no, I'm just an employee, an employee. Ugh.

Pricing Everything: Polymarket and Information Assetization

Chase: There's a project that's been quite popular lately called Polymarket, I'm sure you've heard of it.

Class Representative: I don't know much about it, can you tell us about it? I've actually heard of it, but I don't really understand it. I always thought it sounded like a scam, so I didn't look into it.

Chase: OK, it first became popular during Trump's election. Basically, it's a prediction market, but it's actually a binary options market. It's about knowing the election results a few hours before they're announced. What's its logic? It believes that there's always someone in the world with inside information about something.

Class Representative: Agreed.

Class Representative: Agreed.

Chase: So your inside information should be priced, it should be priced. Class Representative: Right. Chase: This is a market that sets a price for it. Class Representative: Okay, he's not a scammer. That makes sense. Haha, one sentence convinced me. Chase: Haha. That's exactly what happened. I have a pretty good relationship with that founder, we often eat and drink together. Now you can think of it indirectly as, in the context of deglobalization, Crypto is a sector that maintains a very strong global presence. And this sector puts all information on the same network, and all information can be priced. Information can become an asset, traffic can become an asset, any transaction, any idea can become an asset. And as long as this asset has liquidity, it can be absorbed.

The Measurement of AI Economy and the Failure of GDP

Chase: Like I mentioned before, the reason I wrote the AI ​​paper is that I believe the GDP or economic scale generated by agents in the future will definitely exceed the economic scale of humans. At that time, you can't do it without a measurement, without a certain infrastructure. The readily available infrastructure now is Crypto. But in economics, or in academia, there's no index to say what this agent actually looks like. Right now, we can only estimate what AI might look like in the future through Google reports, OpenAI's funding data, Oracle building data centers and factories, and CapEx.

Class Representative: I understand.

Chase: If you think about it using the first principle, it means that I should measure every agent, every transaction, and it should be very precise.

Class Representative: Let me briefly explain this to my channel's viewers. My understanding is that there are two core issues. The first is the actual value of AI, whether it's valuable or not. This is actually very difficult to price. Because if we only look at OpenAI's revenue, it's not much money, a few tens of billion, right? But Dai Yusen raised an idea, and I even conducted a survey at an event: if I bought out your future rights to use ChatGPT, you wouldn't be allowed to use ChatGPT or any new AI. How much would you be willing to pay to have that right bought out? When I asked about $100,000, only one person raised their hand; out of about 100 people, only one person raised their hand. When I asked about $1 million, many people still didn't raise their hands. $1 million—so this thing is extremely valuable, but I can't measure it. The second issue is how much productivity AI actually generates. GDP is actually very difficult to measure. Chase: It's completely wrong about GDP. G stands for gross, right? But it's not actually gross. Every time you make an API call, you can precisely measure it. Second, it's domestic. AI isn't domestic either; it's global. Third, it's not a product, not a finished product; it's in many intermediate states. So every word in GDP is wrong. If you use that to measure AI, that's why we absolutely need a new index, at least a micro-level measurement. And whether we can develop a macro-level index on top of that is also important. We're already writing some; we already have a framework. Class Representative: What I mean is, this macro- and micro-level link—being able to write a framework—but whether OpenAI will give you the measurement is another matter. Chase: Yes, you're absolutely right. This might suggest that when the number of agents reaches a certain level, and one or two killer apps emerge, you might not need OpenAI to manage, care about, or collect your data 100%. You could simply host your own agent locally. Then, when this agent needs to handle complex tasks, you could rely on cloud-based agents. For daily tasks, you can do it from home. I have two MacBooks, a MacBook and a Mac mini, and I can connect them to run a 31B model from Qianwen at home. My data is all local, and it works very well.

Bitcoin 2026 and Market Manipulation: The "Stop Acting" Market Makers

Class Representative: Hmm, we're back to value creation. Let's talk about money first, hahaha. Let's talk about the question everyone cares about most: Will Bitcoin rise or fall in 2026?

... Let's make a prediction. Chase: It will rise. It will definitely rise, it will definitely reach an all-time high. But in a way you probably haven't considered. Class Representative: Why? Chase: Let's go back to my previous topic, all information can be priced. Now, in the crypto world, anyone can be priced within this network. When everyone reaches a consensus, that consensus will be priced, and there will be people who oppose that consensus to cash out you. Okay, this might be a bit deep. If you've ever been a market maker, if you know about quant trading, you'll find it's always a big fish eats the small fish. But in the process of a minority eating the majority, when the majority forms a large consensus, you'll likely become a target. Others will price you as that target. Class Representative: So, when everyone thinks something is going to fall, it rises. It doesn't actually fall. Why is it that when you know it will fall, when you are convinced it will fall, you have already been socially engineered? I'm talking about the general public, not you personally. But this feeling isn't anything new; it's the manipulation logic of stock market manipulators. Chase: Right. Class Representative: Can anyone manipulate the Bitcoin market? It's such a large market. Or is it really that large? Chase: Bitcoin's market capitalization is currently around 2.5 trillion US dollars, so it's not small. It's about 1/10 the size of gold. Last year, around February to April, there was a week where Bitcoin's candlestick chart, if you look at the daily chart, showed that it went up for seven consecutive days with the same amplitude and volume, and then suddenly went down. What does this mean? This kind of pattern usually appears when a strong manipulator can control the entire market. In other words, some people or a group wanted to announce to the world on that day that they had "controlled" this "guys" thing, it's that simple. Class Representative: OK, he not only controlled it, but he directly sent a clear signal. Chase: Right. What does he want? He wants you to join, he wants those who understand to join him and devour those who don't. Class Representative: Wow, I need to check what it is sometime soon, I'll find it for you. Chase: I'll find it for you, wow haha. This industry is very, very interesting. Class Representative: Who is this person? Chase: There's no specific person, and you'll never know who it is. Here's the thing: there are many market makers here, and there's a lot of overlap between traditional financial market makers and crypto market makers. These market makers don't look at fundamentals; they only look at market liquidity and signals they believe in. Once these signals appear, they trigger a trade. This trading method, the situation I just described, falls into one category: either the market liquidity at that time was controlled by a few people using the same algorithm, so everyone corroborated each other's claims, "OK guys, we're all in this together," or they felt that other people with chips or money wouldn't participate, wouldn't enter their game at that time. About a year ago, on a certain day a year ago, I found this for you. [Class Representative: Okay~ I found the screenshot for you. Continuing.] Bitcoin has shown a trend that insiders know is being manipulated by experts. If that's the case, then it's definitely as you said, they can already profit from it, and they know how to do it this way. Just understand. Valuation System: Liquidity, Attention, and Shareholding Structure Class Representative: Next, you just mentioned Bitcoin, which aligns with my understanding. Bitcoin is a store of value on the blockchain, much like a vault where we trade based on gold. The real question is, whether you make a lot of money or lose a lot of money here depends on factors outside of Bitcoin itself. The value of gold, regardless of fluctuations, is relatively stable. Even at its all-time high, it might only be up 30-40% from now. Besides leveraged trading, what else do you see? Chase: This brings us back to what you learned in traditional economics. First, valuation drives the market, right? Second, liquidity drives it, or rather, it's driven by fundamentals. However, the valuation system in the crypto world is different from traditional finance. It mainly consists of three parts. One is liquidity. One is traffic, which is attention. The last is your entire asset structure, which is what we call Tokenomics. These three components almost determine what your asset will look like in the short to medium term. Nobody looks at the long term, nobody looks at the long term.

Class Representative: How short is short to medium term?

Chase: Generally, 7 days to 3 months, you can almost see the general trend of an asset. But, for example, like Richard's project, their project is actually a very, very good project, but in terms of token issuance, because they didn't have experience before, so it wasn't particularly successful. But from a long-term perspective, this project is very, very worthy of everyone's attention. For example, they recently launched Defensive Loopring, a new feature. It indirectly helps you leverage assets that they consider to have very low risk, and then prevents you from suffering losses. It's almost at the smart contract level. What is the result?

That means you can get an annualized return of around 17% to 16%. A dozen points is very, very considerable. And it doesn't have much of an upper limit on the amount of capital you can invest; you can put in a few million and sit back and relax. Chase: That's a good example of why their coins haven't seen a price increase. The underlying assets are already so good, so what causes the price of the coin, or the gap in the middle? Once your coin, this asset, is in circulation, frankly, firstly, you can't control it anymore; it's all market (behavior). Class Representative: Yes. Chase: But because the market, the main participants in this market pricing are either retail investors who don't understand, or market makers who have very large funds but don't care about value; they only care about short-term profits. So this leads to everyone choosing short-term gains and not long-term ones. So when should you enter the market? Especially in situations like yours, or that of a traditional finance professional, or even my current situation, if you want to invest long-term, it's only after the first wave is over, when people realize the token still has value, and its valuation is no longer affected by liquidity and attention. For example, if you look at Richard's project Huma, its current valuation is probably only around 300 million, or about 300 million US dollars. But in my opinion, it's worth at least two billion; it's just waiting for the right opportunity. The hype is basically over now. Class Representative: Right. Chase: Exactly, the speculative wave is over. Class Representative: I think looking at crypto trading, like on Binance, vividly illustrates the stock market's so-called value component, the trading component, and the speculative or market-controlled component, making it very difficult to see clearly. But it's especially obvious and clear in crypto. Chase: So it's all very real, nobody's pretending, nobody's pretending. Class Representative: Right. No pretense. Chase: No pretense. Because it hasn't been defined as security yet, there are very few regulations governing it. So many people start doing bad things, right? It's hard to avoid. Industry Figures: Justin Sun, Tether, and the Truth About Profits Class Representative: Speaking of which, let's introduce someone who doesn't pretend at all. Or maybe he's just acting the whole time. Like the Marquis of Nanlong in the novel *A Record of a Mortal's Journey to Immortality*, he's playing himself, a very cunning person. Justin Sun, you should know a lot about his story. I think he's a good case study. Could you tell us about the characters involved and their underlying motives? It's really confusing; he's everywhere, so it's unclear whether he's genuinely wealthy or just pretending. How do you tell? Can you give us some insider information? Chase: Insider information... Let's talk about Nanlonghou first. After all, he's a descendant of Cangkun Shangren, right? He'd definitely keep things under wraps. In the cultivation world of Wang Yu (the author of *A Record of a Mortal's Journey to Immortality*), it's full of intrigue and backstabbing. You can't help it; that's his protective shield. Even so, he was still betrayed, right? So it's hard to say. As for Sun Ge, I have no personal interaction with him, so I can't really comment on him. But he's genuinely wealthy; that's common knowledge in the industry. Knowing he's wealthy might be a question mark for outsiders, but within the industry, it's 100% certain. Class Representative: So what does the industry know? It should be publicly available information, right? Does it have a publicly available address? Chase: It's a publicly available address on the blockchain. They have a public blockchain called Tron, right? You know USDT, right? Actually, USDT's largest parking space is on Tron. That means Tron and USDT have a symbiotic relationship, at least before. And think about how much currency USDT has printed—I forget if it's 60% or some percentage—I can check now, it's better to share real data. There are currently 4.7 billion (TVL) on Tron. USDT has 80 billion on Tron. What does that mean? It means its largest share is Ethereum at 46.77%, and the second largest is Tron at 43.07%, that's over 80%. Chase: Tether's annual revenue is $15 billion. The net profit is almost... OpenAI loses billions a year, Anthropic loses billions a year, and even combined, they don't earn as much as Tether. You lose less than they earn. Class Representative: Where does Tether's revenue come from? Is it from transaction fees? Chase: No, Tether is the issuer of USDT. The underlying logic is, for example, you give me $1, and I use that dollar to buy T-bill (US Treasury bonds). After buying the T-bill, I give you the USDT token. So for you, you exchanged $1 for a USDT token, right? It's one dollar for one dollar, and you can go out and have fun. But for me, every dollar that comes in, I can earn the interest from the T-bill given to me by the Federal Reserve. So as long as my size is large enough, I'll keep profiting from this 3.5% interest rate. That's all the interest I'm making right now, 15 billion a year.

Class Representative: Haha. Sun is really rich. Shall we continue? Shall we continue?

Chase: Really rich. Haha, I'm not familiar with Sun, but I can give you some links to gossip. It's not good to talk about him in public.

Wealth Distribution and the Metaphor of "Cultivating Immortality"

Class Representative: Hmm...that's true. Shall we talk about the stories of people who got rich overnight in the crypto world? Who are these people? We just talked about the essence or value of this place, and its current state, right? For example, people don't act. It's actually a very clear trading platform. I think its underlying logic is actually quite clear.

So, what kind of people are these people here? Chase: I've observed that in all crypto gatherings, the proportion of beautiful women is exceptionally high. Class Representative: Right, it's amazing. Chase: It's probably a particularly high proportion of gold diggers. This can be verified. (Of course) there are also many good women; more than half of my former colleagues were women, and they were all very outstanding. The crypto industry as a whole is actually a separate world, a separate country. It has everything. This entire Spectrum, from the far left to the far right, has it all. Imagine, on the far right, there's the US president, people with the highest power, and their families involved in all sorts of things in the crypto world. On the far left, there are people in jail who have swindled tens or hundreds of billions of dollars. All kinds of people are there. Class Representative: From my perspective, I'm someone who understands technology, and I've even made a few videos about Web3, so I know a bit about it. But I feel completely outside this circle. Aside from buying one Bitcoin, I have absolutely no connection to it. Occasionally, I see people around me saying they're working in Web3. It seems like only a minority are making money, but some people seem to understand the rules of the game and can rapidly amplify their wealth—the so-called "get rich quick." What percentage of those involved do you think truly understand this game? Chase: First of all, the barrier to entry isn't high. If you're diligent enough, even a high school student—not to mention in China, but even in China, if you know how to use a VPN—can easily earn tens of thousands of RMB a month. Class Representative: Through what means? Trading or something else? Chase: By participating in new projects, like airdrops, right? It's actually a part of monetizing traffic.
